critic_raw:
The primary evaluation metric is precision, chosen because stakeholders want to minimize false positives (unnecessary re-inspections). But the class distribution in defect detection datasets is highly imbalanced—defects are rare. A model that predicts 'no defect' for every image achieves perfect precision on the majority class and undefined precision on the defect class (no positive predictions). Precision on the defect class alone collapses to undefined when a model predicts no positives, which a conservative threshold would do. The design must specify which class's precision is being optimized and at what threshold; without this, the primary metric cannot be operationalized.

The stratified random split on images from an operational shipping facility introduces temporal leakage. Container defects at shipping facilities are not IID over time: inspection protocol changes, new container types, seasonal traffic patterns, and inspector-specific biases introduce temporal structure. The design acknowledges 'seasonal variations and potential changes in inspection protocols' as confounds, but the stratification-by-time-period it proposes does not prevent train/test contamination—it only checks whether the contamination differs across periods. A proper temporal hold-out split is not described.

The baseline is described as 'traditional image processing techniques' but never specified. It is impossible to assess whether the comparison is fair or meaningful without knowing the specific technique—Canny edge detection, Otsu thresholding, morphological filtering, or a hand-engineered SVM on HOG features are all 'traditional' but differ by orders of magnitude in capability. The baseline is a black box.

For a dataset sourced from a single operational facility, the model will learn facility-specific visual characteristics: the lighting rig, the camera model, the container color palette, the background structure. This is not a flaw the experiment can resolve internally—it is an external validity problem. The design offers no cross-facility or cross-camera validation, meaning reported precision/recall numbers cannot be interpreted as estimates of general-purpose defect detection capability.

The design says the CNN is 'tailored for industrial anomaly detection' but does not specify whether it uses supervised learning or an anomaly detection objective (e.g., reconstruction-based, one-class classification). For severely imbalanced defect data, these are architecturally different approaches with different theoretical guarantees, and conflating them under 'CNN for anomaly detection' makes the experimental conditions underspecified.
all_issues_raised: Precision as the primary metric is undefined when the model predicts no positives (which a conservative threshold on imbalanced data naturally produces); the design does not specify class-specific precision or threshold policy.

Stratified random split on images from a facility with known temporal structure (protocol changes, seasonal variation) introduces temporal leakage; the time-period stratification check described does not prevent contamination, it only measures it post hoc.
The traditional image processing baseline is never specified, making it impossible to assess comparability; 'traditional techniques' spans several orders of magnitude in capability.

The model is trained and tested on a single facility, encoding facility-specific visual artifacts; no cross-facility validation is described, so the results cannot generalize beyond this specific camera-lighting-container configuration.

The CNN is described simultaneously as a supervised classifier and as an anomaly detection model without clarifying which objective is used; these have different theoretical properties and appropriate evaluation protocols.
all_issues_adjudicated: Precision as the primary metric is undefined when the model predicts no positives (which a conservative threshold on imbalanced data naturally produces); the design does not specify class-specific precision or threshold policy.

Stratified random split on images from a facility with known temporal structure (protocol changes, seasonal variation) introduces temporal leakage; the time-period stratification check described does not prevent contamination, it only measures it post hoc.
The traditional image processing baseline is never specified, making it impossible to assess comparability; 'traditional techniques' spans several orders of magnitude in capability.
